elements.sent-2411-001: The effectiveness of Large Language Models (LLMs) in solving tasks vastly depends on the quality of the instructions, which often require fine-tuning through extensive human effort.
elements.sent-2411-002: This highlights the need for automated instruction optimization; however, this optimization is particularly challenging when dealing with black-box LLMs, where model parameters and gradients remain inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose ACING, a task-specific prompt optimization approach framed as a stateless continuous-action Reinforcement Learning (RL) problem, known as the continuum bandit setting.
elements.sent-2411-004: ACING leverages an actor-critic-based method to optimize prompts, learning from non-differentiable reward signals.
elements.sent-2411-005: We validate ACING by optimizing prompts for ChatGPT on 30 instruction-based tasks, as well as a summarization task.
elements.sent-2411-006: ACING consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving a median score improvement of 10 percentage points compared to the best baseline considered.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, ACING not only recovers but also surpasses human-crafted expert instructions, achieving up to a 39 percentage point improvement over human benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks \cite{zhao2023survey, touvron2023llama .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This success is largely attributed to their strong instruction-following capabilities, which enable adaptation to diverse downstream applications \cite{chen2023you, liu2023prompt-survey .}
elements.sent-2411-010: These instructions, commonly referred to as prompts, play a crucial role in the performance of LLMs \cite{wei2022chain, zhu2023promptbench, liu2023prompt-survey .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Given the importance of prompts, researchers are increasingly interested in automating their optimization to reduce the need for manual adjustments, which is often a labor-intensive and costly process \cite{reynolds2021manual, mishra2021reframing .}
elements.sent-2411-012: As a result, developing efficient methods for automatic prompt optimization has become highly important to maximize LLMs performance.
elements.sent-2411-013: Several methods for automated prompt optimization have been proposed in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-014: Some focus on optimizing continuous prompts, known as soft prompts \cite{zhong2021factual, li2021prefix , which are typically fed into a LLM after its embedding layer since they do not represent meaningful language that the LLM can interpret directly.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Other methods aim at optimizing discrete prompts, known as hard prompts, by either generating diverse prompts \cite{zhou2023large or refining existing ones \cite{pryzant2023automatic .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: For both soft and hard prompts, some approaches rely on gradient-based methods \cite{shin2020autoprompt, lester2021power, li2021prefix .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, these methods require access to the gradients of the LLMs and are therefore limited to white-box LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-018: Meanwhile, the most powerful LLMs today are typically black-box models (e.g., ChatGPT \cite{openai2023chatgpt and GPT-4 \cite{openai2023gpt4 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-019: To overcome these challenges, recent studies have introduced gradient-free techniques for optimizing prompts in black-box LLMs \cite{zhou2023large, prasad2022grips, pryzant2023automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-020: While promising, these approaches rely on heuristic local search methods and fail to fully utilize the observation history, including previously tested instructions and their outcomes, when selecting new instructions to test.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, they are less effective at balancing the inherent exploration-exploitation trade-off, leading to inefficient querying.
elements.sent-2411-022: This inefficiency becomes problematic and impractical when API calls to black-box LLMs involve significant time and financial costs.
elements.sent-2411-023: A recent line of work has focused on optimizing instructions for black-box LLMs by searching through soft prompts and transforming them into discrete prompts using white-box LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-024: InstructZero \cite{instruct optimizes discrete prompts for ChatGPT by applying Bayesian optimization over soft prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Building on the same line, INSTINCT \cite{instinct replaces Bayesian optimization with NeuralUCB \cite{zhou2020neural , a critic algorithm, to select actions, from a finite pool of soft prompts, initially generated with Sobol \cite{sobol .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Inspired by the success of actor-critic methods for control problems \cite{haarnoja2018soft, haarnoja2018softapp and generative adversarial networks \cite{goodfellow2014generative , our approach combines the hybrid white-box/black-box framework with an actor-critic Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm, operating in a stateless, infinite continuous-action space, and constrained by a strict budget of API calls.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The actor aims to maximize the critic's value while remaining as stochastic as possible to enhance exploration and simultaneously exploit promising actions.
elements.sent-2411-028: This approach increases the likelihood of finding optimal prompts and enables more efficient use of the limited number of API calls.
elements.sent-2411-029: ACING consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving a median score improvement of 10 percentage points.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, our optimized prompts not only recover but also surpass human-crafted expert instructions, achieving up to a 39 percentage point improvement against human benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-031: An implementation of ACING is available at \url{https://github.com/salmakh1/ACING .}
elements.sent-2411-032: We aim to improve the performance of a black-box LLM, denoted by $f$, which can only be accessed through its API, while its internal parameters remain unknown.
elements.sent-2411-033: Given a task represented by an (unknown) distribution $(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}$—where $x$ denotes possible inputs and $y$ the corresponding correct outputs—our goal is to find the optimal prompt $\tau^\star$ that maximizes the likelihood of $f$ producing correct outputs for a given task.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is evaluated using a scoring function $q(\cdot, \cdot) \in [0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The black-box model $f$ processes an input formed by concatenating ($\oplus$) the prompt $\tau$ with the sentence $x$, producing a predicted output $\hat{y = f(\tau \oplus x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: More formally, the objective is to maximize the expected score of the LLM in solving the task $\mathcal{D}$, defined as: \begin{equation}\label{discrete_problem} \max_\tau \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: To achieve this, we utilize a validation dataset $\mathcal{V = \{(x_j, y_j)\}_{j=1 ^{m}$, where each pair consists of an input sentence $x_j$ and its corresponding ground truth output $y_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Our objective is to find the prompt that maximizes the scoring function $q(\cdot, \cdot)$ across the validation dataset, where $q(\hat{y _j, y_j)$ measures the quality of the predicted output $\hat{y _j$ against the true output $y_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, our objective becomes finding the prompt $\tau^\star$ that maximizes the average score over the validation set $\mathcal{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-040: The derived prompt $\tau^\star$ is then evaluated on a separate test set $\mathcal{T = \{(x^\prime_j, y^\prime_j)\}_{j=1 ^{m^\prime}$ to assess its generalization performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Directly optimizing the prompt $\tau$ for the black-box LLM model $f$ presents substantial challenges due to the discrete combinatorial nature of token selection in $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-042: To mitigate this challenge, prior approaches like InstructZero \cite{instruct and INSTINCT \cite{instinct employ a white-box LLM, represented by $h$, to convert the discrete optimization problem into a continuous one by introducing a soft prompt vector $\mathbf{z \in \mathbb{R ^d$, which is a continuous $d$-dimensional vector representing the token embedding of a set of virtual tokens.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The white-box model $h$ has access to a dataset of exemplars, $\mathcal{E = \{ (u_j, v_j) \}_{j=1 ^k$, where each pair $(u_j, v_j)$ defines input-output text sequences that exemplify a downstream task.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The white-box LLM $h$ serves as a proxy, mapping the soft prompt $\mathbf{z}$ into a discrete prompt $\tau$ suitable for input to the black-box LLM.
elements.sent-2411-045: Specifically, given the exemplars $\mathcal{E}$ and the vector $\mathbf{z}$, their concatenation is input to the white-box LLM, generating the discrete prompt $\tau(\mathbf{z ) = h(\mathbf{z , \mathcal{E )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: This generated prompt $\tau(\mathbf{z )$ is prepended to a test input $x_j$ from the validation set $\mathcal{V}$, and the combined input is provided to the black-box LLM $f$ to generate an output $\hat{y _j = f(\tau(\mathbf{z ) \oplus x_j)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The output $\hat{y _j$ is then evaluated against the true output $y_j$ using the scoring function $q(\hat{y _j, y_j)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: By using a fixed set of exemplars $\mathcal{E}$, the original discrete problem (Equation (\ref{discrete_problem )) of finding the optimal prompt $\tau$ is effectively transformed into a continuous optimization problem over the soft prompt vector $\mathbf{z}$, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{continious_problem} \max_{\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^d} \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau(\mathbf{z}) \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The soft prompt $\mathbf{z}$ is typically high-dimensional (e.g., $d = 5120 \times N_z$, with $N_z$ the number of virtual tokens, when $h$ is Vicuna-13B~\cite{vicuna2023 ), which makes it a challenging problem.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, we employ random projection techniques to reduce the input dimension as done in prior works \cite{instruct, instinct .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Specifically, given a matrix $P \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d^{\prime }$ where $d^{\prime \ll d$, with randomly sampled elements, and a continuous vector $\mathbf{a \in \mathbb{R ^{d^{\prime }$, the vector $\mathbf{z = P\mathbf{a}$ is used as the soft prompt.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: By substituting $\mathbf{z}$ with $P\mathbf{a}$, the input variable to be optimized in Eq. \ref{continious_problem becomes $\mathbf{a}$, thereby reducing the input dimension of the optimization problem to $d^{\prime}$: \begin{equation}\label{continious_problem_} \max_{\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{d^{'}}} \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau(P\mathbf{a}) \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-053: For fairness with previous works, the reduced input dimension $d^{\prime}$, referred to as the intrinsic dimension, is chosen as $d^{\prime = 10$ \cite{instruct, instinct .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: An ablation study on the intrinsic dimension is discussed in \S~\ref{diff_dimensions .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Furthermore, to maintain consistency with these prior studies, we similarly use a projection matrix $P$ with elements uniformly and independently sampled from $\text{Uniform (-1,1)$, and bound the vector such that $\mathbf{a \in [0,1]^{d'}$.}}
elements.section-2411-003: Actor-Critic Approach for Instruction Learning in Black-Box LLMs
elements.subsection-2411-004: Instruction Learning as an RL Problem
elements.sent-2411-056: In this work, we frame the prompt-learning problem as an agent navigating an infinite action space within a $d^{'}$-dimensional unit hypercube, searching within an infinite number of actions $\mathbf{a \in [0,1]^{d^{' }$, in contrast to previous works that consider a finite number of actions initially generated with Sobol \cite{sobol, instinct .}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: As part of the environment, these continuous actions are projected into a higher-dimensional space (\S~\ref{projection_section ) and then transformed into discrete prompts, through a white-box LLM (\S~\ref{Methodology ).}}
elements.sent-2411-058: These discrete prompts are evaluated by a black-box LLM over a validation dataset, after which a reward is computed and returned to the agent.
elements.sent-2411-059: We consider a finite horizon \( T \), representing the maximum number of actions played (i.e., the budget of evaluated instructions), after which the agent ceases exploration.
elements.sent-2411-060: The agent's goal is to discover the optimal continuous action, $\mathbf{a ^\star$, which produces the optimal discrete prompt and the highest reward.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Unlike in general RL settings, where the agent encounters multiple states with varying reward distributions, the agent we consider operates in a stateless, stochastic environment, commonly referred to as a stochastic bandit setting \cite{slivkins2019introduction, lattimore2020bandit .}
elements.sent-2411-062: In this context, at each iteration $t \leq T$ (where $t$ is incremented with every black-box LLM call), the agent's action $\mathbf{a _t$ generate stochastic rewards, $r_t \in [0,1]$, without affecting the reward distributions associated with those actions.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The prompt-learning objective is modeled as a stochastic best-arm identification problem in a stateless, continuous action space RL, specifically in an infinite continuum bandit setting.
elements.sent-2411-064: RL techniques are generally used to learn optimal policies and actions leading to best rewards.
elements.sent-2411-065: These techniques can be classified into value-based (critic-centric), policy-gradient (actor-centric), or hybrid approaches.
elements.sent-2411-066: Actor-critic algorithms merge the benefits of both by employing two key components: the actor, responsible for policy optimization, and the critic, which evaluates the policy \citep{sutton2018reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-067: This hybrid approach has demonstrated success in various domains, including continuous control problems \cite{pmlr-v48-mniha16, haarnoja2018soft, haarnoja2018softapp and with generative adversarial networks (GANs) \citep{goodfellow2014generative , where a generator and a critic network collaboratively optimize performance.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.38]{figures/steps+templates.pdf} \caption{ Pipeline of ACING. In each iteration, a soft prompt along with several examples of the target task are provided to the white-box LLM to generate an instruction. This instruction is then used to query the black-box LLM, which produces answers to the target task queries. The resulting score is returned to the agent as a reward, which is used to update its networks and adjust its policy. Both LLMs remain frozen throughout the process. } \label{fig:ACING}
elements.sent-2411-068: Inspired by the success of actor-critic approaches, we introduce a (stateless) actor-critic algorithm, as provided in Algorithm~\ref{alg:ssac , tailored to our infinite continuum bandit setting, enabling autonomous learning of effective prompts with a constrained evaluation budget and outperforming previous state-of-the-art black-box prompt learning approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Both the actor and the critic are parameterized by neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-070: In typical RL problems, the agent interacts with an environment with varying states, so its policy depends on the current state.
elements.sent-2411-071: That is, the policy network generates actions based on the current state $s$, $\pi(.| s; \theta)$, where $\theta$ denotes the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, in the problem we consider, the setting is stateless.
elements.sent-2411-073: Therefore, we define a policy network $\pi(.; \theta)$ with a constant input that produces a $d^{'}$-dimensional vector as output $\mathbf{a}$, representing the continuous action.
elements.sent-2411-074: For the critic, we use a neural network with a $d^{'}$-dimensional input (the action) and a single output that estimates the action's quality, thereby assessing the quality of the policy that proposed it.
elements.sent-2411-075: The critic network's parameters are trained to minimize the following: \begin{equation} \label{critic_loss} \min_{\mathbf{w}} J_Q(\mathbf{w}) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{a} \sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}\right)-r\left(\mathbf{a}\right) \right)^2\right],\end{equation} which can be optimized with stochastic gradients \begin{equation} \label{critic_gradient} \hat{\nabla}_\theta J_Q(\mathbf{w})=\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)\left(Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)-r\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)\right),\end{equation} where $Q$ is the critic-network with parameters $\mathbf{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The actor-critic learning process alternates between policy-network and critic-network updates.
elements.sent-2411-077: In general, RL learning approaches aim to maximize the expected cumulative rewards up to a horizon \( T \), expressed as $ \max_{\pi(\cdot) \sum_{t=1 ^T \mathbb{E _{a_t \sim \pi(\cdot) \left[ r(a_t) \right].$ In contrast, black-box prompt learning, framed as a best-arm identification problem, seeks the best prompt without necessarily maximizing the expected cumulative rewards.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: To maximize the cumulative rewards, the agent must manage the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-079: Conversely, for the best-arm identification problem, the agent should explore as extensively as possible within the \( T \) evaluation budget to potentially identify the best action, leading to a pure exploration setting.
elements.sent-2411-080: Although it might seem that the agent should act purely at random to find the best action, some degree of exploitation may still be beneficial to guide the agent toward the optimal choice.
elements.sent-2411-081: To achieve a high exploration rate while exploiting prior data, we consider the maximum entropy objective, as discussed in previous RL works \cite{ziebart2010modeling, haarnoja2018soft .}
elements.sent-2411-082: This objective promotes stochastic policies by augmenting the reward with an entropy term, expressed as: \begin{equation} \label{policy_loss} \min_{\theta} J_\pi(\theta) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{a} \sim \pi(.;\theta)}\left[\alpha \log \left(\pi\left(\mathbf{a}; \theta\right)\right)-Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a})\right)\right],\end{equation} where the temperature \( \alpha \) controls the stochasticity of the agent.
elements.sent-2411-083: The above objective is a simplification of the objective in the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm \cite{haarnoja2018soft , eliminating the state, as we consider a stateless environment and focus on instantaneous rather than cumulative rewards.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The term $-\mathbb{E _{a \sim \pi(.;\theta) \left[ \log \pi(a) \right]$ represents the entropy of the policy, which we seek to maximize alongside the critic values, as entropy maximization is crucial for encouraging the actor to explore different actions.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: A high $\alpha$ encourages exploration, whereas a lower value of $\alpha$ favors exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-086: Furthermore, to avoid fine-tuning, and to achieve adaptive exploration, the entropy coefficient $\alpha$ can be adjusted dynamically to maintain a target entropy level \cite{haarnoja2018softapp , $H_{\text{target }$, ensuring sufficient exploration, as follows: \begin{equation} \label{alpha_loss} \min_{\alpha} \mathbf{J}_{\alpha} \triangleq -\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(.)} \left[ \alpha \cdot \left( \log \pi(\mathbf{a};\theta) + H_{\text{target}} \right) \right].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: We use a stochastic approximation of gradients, specifically the Adam algorithm \cite{kingma2014adam, reddi2019convergence , to minimize the losses via stochastic gradient descent.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The learning rates $\lambda$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ are used for the Q-network, the policy networks, and the temperature, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{figures/animal_example.pdf} \caption{ Illustration of the prompt generation and testing inside the environment.} \label{fig:animal_ACING}
elements.sent-2411-089: The methodology of our proposed algorithm, ACING, is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING , showing the actor-critic interaction with the environment.}
elements.sent-2411-090: In Fig.~\ref{fig:animal_ACING , the environment is zoomed in, using the larger\_animal dataset as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-091: In the following, we provide a detailed explanation of the full methodology.. Overview.
elements.sent-2411-092: In each iteration \( t \leq T\), the actor-critic agent generates a continuous vector ``action'' \(\mathbf{a}\) (step 1).
elements.sent-2411-093: This action is then played on the environment, which projects \(\mathbf{a}\) into the appropriate space using a fixed projection matrix $P$ to obtain \(\mathbf{z}\).
elements.sent-2411-094: The environment then concatenates the projected vector \(\mathbf{z}\) with a set of exemplars' embeddings from \(\mathcal{E}\) and feeds it into a white-box LLM $h$ (step 2).
elements.sent-2411-095: The white-box LLM produces a discrete prompt, \(\tau\), which is evaluated using the validation dataset \(\mathcal{V}\) based on the responses from the black-box LLM $f$ (step 3).
elements.sent-2411-096: The black-box LLM generates a prediction, which is then contrasted to the true labels of the validation examples, and a score function provides a final reward to the critic.
elements.sent-2411-097: This reward is used to compute the critic's loss and update both the critic and actor networks accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-098: The policy-network (actor) outputs a mean and variance of a distribution from which the action is sampled.
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, the action, represented as a soft prompt vector, $\mathbf{a}$ $\in$ $\mathbb{R ^{d^{' }$, is obtained by sampling from a Gaussian distribution with the outputted parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The network also computes the associated log probability, which is crucial for policy optimization, as it guides the learning process by balancing exploration and exploitation, by controlling the policy loss, as shown in Eq.~(\ref{policy_loss ).}
elements.sent-2411-101: As depicted in the left side of Fig.~\ref{fig:animal_ACING , the examples describing the task from the set of exemplars $\mathcal{E}$, along with additional text such as ``The instruction was to,'' are input into the embedding layer of the white-box LLM to generate continuous vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-102: These continuous vectors are then concatenated with the soft prompt $\mathbf{z}$, projected from the action $\mathbf{a}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The layers of the white-box LLM subsequently process the resulting concatenated vector to produce the discrete prompt $\tau$ (using the instruction generation template depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING top right).}
elements.sent-2411-104: This transformation is essential for converting the continuous prompt into a discrete format suitable for input into the black-box LLM.
elements.sent-2411-105: As depicted in the right side of Fig.~\ref{fig:animal_ACING , for every input $x_i$ in the validation set $\mathcal{V = \{(x_j, y_j)\}_{j=1 ^{m}$, the generated prompt $\tau$ is concatenated to the input sentence $x_i$ and fed to the black-box LLM, which generates an output sentence $\hat{y _i = f(\tau(\mathbf{z ) \oplus x_i)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The output of the black-box LLM is fed into a scoring function $q(\cdot, \cdot)$, which computes the score between the predicted output $\hat{y _i$ and the true label $y_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The overall score is calculated by averaging the scores across all samples, representing the reward: $r = \frac{1 {m \sum_{i=1 ^m q(\hat{y _i, y_i)$, where $m$ represents the number of samples and $r$ serves as feedback for the actor-critic algorithm.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The critic is responsible for evaluating the quality of actions taken by the actor using the network $Q_{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-109: This network estimates the expected reward for a given action $\mathbf{a}$, which is generated by the policy network $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Observing the reward $r(\mathbf{a )$, the critic computes the loss Eq.~(\ref{critic_loss ) and updates its network using Eq.~(\ref{critic_gradient ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The critic provides feedback to the actor by estimating the Q-values of the actions sampled by the policy, and the actor uses this feedback to improve its policy by maximizing Eq.~(\ref{policy_loss ).}
elements.sent-2411-112: This continuous interaction between the actor and critic ensures the actor learns to take actions that maximize the expected reward while balancing exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-113: By repeating steps 1 to 4 until the allowed budget $T$ is exhausted, the agent returns the best instruction prompt, denoted as $\tau^\star$.
elements.sent-2411-114: This prompt is then evaluated using the test set $\mathcal{T}$ on the black-box LLM, yielding the final score $\mathcal{S ^\star = \frac{1 {m^{\prime \sum_{i=1 ^{m^{\prime q(f(\tau^\star, x^\prime_i), y^\prime_i)$ (using the evaluation template depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING bottom right).}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-115: We tackle instruction learning for ChatGPT.
elements.sent-2411-116: We conduct instruction induction tasks using 30 datasets from~\cite{honovich-etal-2023-instruction, instruct , along with a data summarization task from \cite{gliwa2019samsum .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Furthermore, we compare the best-learned instructions from our ACING algorithm against human-provided instructions from \cite{honovich-etal-2023-instruction , as shown in Table \ref{tab:human_performance_main , with additional best-learned instructions in Table \ref{tab:human_performance_appendix .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Moreover, we compare ACING with three recent representative baselines for black-box instruction learning: APE~\citep{zhou2023large , InstructZero~\citep{instruct , and INSTINCT~\cite{instinct , with results provided in Table \ref{tab:instruction_induction .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Following previous works and to ensure fairness, we do not fine-tune the budget.
elements.sent-2411-120: Instead, like previous works, we consider a fixed budget of black-box LLM evaluations, set to $T = 165$ \cite{instruct, instinct .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Moreover, like previous works, we use Vicuna-13B \cite{vicuna2023 as the white-box LLM $h$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Furthermore, for previous works, we use their default (tuned) hyperparameters, including the intrinsic dimension $d' = 10$ and the number of soft tokens $N_z = 5$.
elements.sent-2411-123: For fairness, we refrain from fine-tuning these parameters for our method and use the same values as in prior works.
elements.sent-2411-124: This ensures that our ACING algorithm searches in the same space, $[0,1]^{10}$, and uses the same total number of queries to the black-box LLM as APE, InstructZero, and INSTINCT for a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-125: For each algorithm, after identifying the best instruction using the validation set $\mathcal{V}$, we evaluate the discovered instruction on a separate test set $\mathcal{T}$ and report the test accuracy as the score.
elements.sent-2411-126: We further conduct ablation studies on the key design choices for ACING, including the use of small learning budgets, providing reward plots for various selected tasks.
elements.sent-2411-127: Furthermore, we test the impact of using different white-box language models, intrinsic dimensions, and numbers of exemplars.

elements.sent-2411-151: Due to the stochastic nature of the black-box LLM, the same instruction may yield different rewards when evaluated by the LLM.

elements.sent-2411-153: In the latter phase, the top \( p \) prompts discovered by the actor-critic are evaluated multiple times, \( k \) times each, using the black-box LLM.
elements.sent-2411-154: Specifically, the first phase uses \( T - p \cdot k \) API calls, while the second uses the remaining calls.
elements.sent-2411-155: The prompt with the highest average reward across repetitions can then be used at test time.
elements.sent-2411-156: In Table \ref{tab:instruction_induction_app in the Appendix, we show that ACING, using this budget split technique with an initial exploration budget of $150$ and a remaining budget of $15$ calls (allocated to uniformly explore the top \( p=5 \) prompts, each evaluated \( k=3 \) times), achieves improved median scores across tasks, resulting in 10 percentage points improvement over the best baseline considered and higher test accuracy on 15 tasks compared to other methods.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Using a Different Number of Exemplars
elements.sent-2411-170: In the main paper, we present results using five exemplars, following the setup of prior work.
elements.sent-2411-171: To evaluate the performance of ACING with a smaller number of exemplars, we conducted an experiment using a single exemplar, i.e., $|\mathcal{E | = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Results shown in Table \ref{tab:instruction_induction_exemplars confirm that using five exemplars generally yield higher test scores and a greater number of best-performing tasks than a single exemplar.}
elements.sent-2411-173: However, a single exemplar still performs well, matching the performance of five exemplars across several tasks (e.g., phonetics, summation, morpho-syntax, translation) and sometimes even outperforming them.
elements.sent-2411-174: Nonetheless, five exemplars offer an advantage, particularly for more cognitively complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-175: Many early approaches such as AutoPrompt \cite{shin2020autoprompt , FluentPrompt \cite{shi2022toward , and other soft prompt-based methods \cite{lester2021power, li2021prefix, zhong2021factual focused on optimizing prompts for white-box LLMs and cannot be directly applied to black-box LLMs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Moreover, several techniques have also been proposed for optimizing prompts for grey-box LLMs: BBT \cite{sun2022black , BBTv2 \cite{sun2022bbtv2 , and clip-tuning \cite{chai2022clip use evolutionary algorithms for this purpose.}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: However, these methods still require access to input token embeddings and output logits, making them unsuitable for settings where only query access is allowed.
elements.sent-2411-178: Other approaches \cite{li2021prefix, zhong2021factual employ continuous soft prompts by fine-tuning parameters of specific input tokens, but these often generate human-unreadable prompts and are impractical for API-accessible LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Some other methods \cite{deng2022rlprompt, zhangtempera , created for optimizing discrete prompts instead of continuous ones, can optimize discrete tokens but require access to the LLM's output distribution, which is typically unavailable in black-box LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-180: However, these methods usually require a large number of queries.
elements.sent-2411-181: Instruction optimization in black-box LLMs recently focused on searching through the space of soft prompts, then relying on open-source white-box LLMs as a means to transform these soft prompts into discrete prompts.
elements.sent-2411-182: InstructZero \cite{instruct learns discrete prompts for ChatGPT by searching through the continuous space of soft prompts using Bayesian optimization, which are then transformed into discrete prompts by a white-box LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-183: INSTINCT \cite{instinct , a successor to this approach, relies on the same approach.}
elements.sent-2411-184: However, instead of Bayesian optimization, it uses NeuralUCB \cite{zhou2020neural , a contextual bandit (selection) algorithm that searches within a finite set of continuous actions generated using Sobol \cite{sobol .}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Our approach combines the hybrid strategy of using white-box and black-box LLMs with an actor-critic approach that operates in a stateless, infinite continuous-action space.
elements.sent-2411-186: TRIPLE \cite{shi2024best modeled the prompt learning problem as a best-arm-identification problem.}
elements.sent-2411-187: However, it only considered the problem as a selection task, assuming a given pool of discrete prompts.
elements.sent-2411-188: In contrast, our method addresses both the generation and selection of prompts.
elements.sent-2411-189: ACING presents a promising approach for learning prompts for black-box LLMs by framing it as a stateless continuous-action RL problem.
elements.sent-2411-190: Our method shows significant improvements over recent baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-191: Evaluations across 31 datasets reveal that ACING enhances prompt quality, achieving a median score improvement of 10 percentage points and outperforming human-crafted instructions by up to 39 percentage points.
elements.sent-2411-192: This sets a new benchmark for automated instruction optimization in black-box LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-193: While our proposed results were achieved using default and commonly used values for hyperparameters in network design, learning rates, and following previous works for some common parameters, the results presented depend on these choices.
elements.sent-2411-194: These results could be further improved with fine-tuning, as demonstrated in the ablation studies (\S~\ref{ablation_main ).}
elements.sent-2411-195: Another limitation of this work, similar to previous studies, is the risk of overfitting when dealing with a limited number of validation examples.
elements.sent-2411-196: This can result in a prompt that minimizes the loss on the validation set but does not generalize well to the test dataset, leading to a discrepancy between training and test performance.
elements.sent-2411-197: Another limitation of this work, similar to previous work \citep{instinct, instruct , is the reliance on a white-box model, where the choice of model may lead to performance variations, as demonstrated in \S~\ref{diff_white_box_main .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Avoiding this limitation requires acting directly on the black-box LLM, which involves handling actions in a large discrete action space.
elements.sent-2411-199: This necessitates adapting current RL approaches for large discrete action spaces, as studied in \cite{pmlr-v235-fourati24a and the references therein.. \onecolumn .}
elements.sent-2411-200: Across the diverse tasks, in the main paper, the same hyperparameters were used, which shows that the algorithm generalizes well across the 30 tasks without specifically tuning hyperparameters in each task.

elements.section-2411-013: ACING with Different Number of Exemplars
elements.sent-2411-223: In this section, we test ACING with a single exemplar, in contrast to the main results in the paper, which use five exemplars for ACING and all other benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-224: For these experiments, we fix all hyperparameters as in the main paper and run tests with a budget of $165$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Intuitively, providing more exemplars to the language model should facilitate prompt learning, so five exemplars are expected to yield better prompts than a single exemplar.
elements.sent-2411-226: Our experiments, summarized in Table \ref{tab:instruction_induction_exemplars , support this intuition.}
elements.sent-2411-227: The results show that using five exemplars leads to higher test scores, as reflected in a greater number of best-performing tasks and an increase in median test scores across tasks.
elements.sent-2411-228: However, it is notable that performance did not decrease drastically with only one exemplar, suggesting that a single exemplar is sufficient to achieve decent results.
elements.sent-2411-229: In fact, across several tasks and categories (e.g., phonetics, summation, morpho-syntax, and translation), a single exemplar achieves the same performance of using five exemplars, and even outperforms the use of five exemplars in certain tasks.
elements.sent-2411-230: Nevertheless, using a single exemplar resulted in lower performance mainly in more cognitively challenging tasks, which is understandable, as more complex tasks are likely to benefit from additional exemplars.

elements.sent-2411-236: The effectiveness of Large Language Models (LLMs) in solving tasks depends significantly on the quality of their instructions, which often require substantial human effort to craft.
elements.sent-2411-237: This underscores the need for automated instruction optimization.
elements.sent-2411-238: However, optimizing instructions is particularly challenging when working with black-box LLMs, where model parameters and gradients are inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-239: We introduce ACING, an actor-critic reinforcement learning framework that formulates instruction optimization as a stateless, continuous-action problem, enabling exploration of infinite instruction spaces using only black-box feedback.
elements.sent-2411-240: ACING automatically discovers prompts that outperform human-written prompts in 76\% of instruction-induction tasks, with gains of up to 33 points and a 10-point median improvement over the best automatic baseline in 33 tasks spanning instruction-induction, summarization, and chain-of-thought reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-241: Extensive ablations highlight its robustness and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-242: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across tasks like classification, summarization, and reasoning \cite{cobbe2021training, touvron2023llama, zhao2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-243: A key driver of this success is their ability to follow natural language instructions, commonly known as prompts \cite{chen2023you, wei2022chain, liu2023prompt-survey .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Yet crafting effective prompts remains labor-intensive and brittle, particularly in black-box settings where model internals are inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-245: A core challenge in prompt engineering lies in the extreme sensitivity of LLMs to subtle linguistic variations.
elements.sent-2411-246: Even minor changes in phrasing can lead to substantial performance shifts.
elements.sent-2411-247: As shown in \cref{tab:prompt_sensitivity , a minor wording change yields a 12-point gain (using GPT-4o \cite{hurst2024gpt ) despite preserving semantics.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Such prompt sensitivity is pervasive, underscoring the need for robust, automated prompt optimization methods.
elements.sent-2411-249: Recent work has explored automating prompt design to reduce human effort \cite{reynolds2021manual, mishra2021reframing .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Soft prompting techniques \cite{li2021prefix, lester2021power and heuristic-based discrete search methods \cite{zhou2023large, pryzant2023automatic have shown promise, yet each faces key limitations in black-box settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Soft prompts require access to the model internals, limiting them to white-box scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-252: Discrete search methods, in turn, often struggle to explore vast and nuanced instruction spaces efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-253: More recent hybrid methods combine white-box prompt generation with black-box evaluation \cite{chen2023instructzero, instinct , but typically rely on finite candidate pools or rigid reward assumptions, constraining their applicability.}
elements.sent-2411-254: To overcome these limitations, we introduce ACING, an actor-critic reinforcement learning (RL) framework for automated instruction optimization in black-box LLM settings.
elements.sent-2411-255: ACING formulates prompt optimization as a stateless, continuous-action RL problem within a continuum bandit environment.
elements.sent-2411-256: By learning a latent instruction space through an off-policy actor-critic algorithm, ACING efficiently explores infinite instruction candidates, using only black-box feedback for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-257: A frozen white-box model is used as a decoder to convert latent vectors into discrete prompts, enabling both scalability and linguistic richness.
elements.sent-2411-258: The generated instructions are not only high-performing but also naturally interpretable, clear, and semantically aligned with the tasks, making them suitable for practical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-259: To effectively guide exploration under tight query budgets, ACING leverages entropy-regularized policy optimization, encouraging diversity in sampled prompts and improving the likelihood of discovering high-performing instructions.
elements.figure-2411-014: 0.45\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{clc} Source Instruction Score Human Write a word that means the opposite of the input word. 0.70 ACING Take a word and change it to its opposite. 0.82 \end{tabular}} \caption{ Our prompt vs human on GPT-4o for antonym task.} \label{tab:prompt_sensitivity}
elements.sent-2411-260: Notably, to our knowledge, ACING is the first approach to apply off-policy, continuous-action actor-critic reinforcement learning to instruction learning in black-box LLMs, using lightweight actor and critic neural networks (rather than LLMs), making the approach both efficient and widely applicable.
elements.sent-2411-261: We evaluate ACING against state-of-the-art techniques, including Bayesian optimization, contextual bandits, and evolutionary strategies.
elements.sent-2411-262: Empirical results across 33 diverse tasks—including instruction induction, reasoning (including zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT)), semantics, syntax, phonetics, translation, summarization, and code understanding—show that ACING outperforms both human-written prompts and strong automated baselines.
elements.sent-2411-263: Notably, it surpasses human instructions in 76\% of instruction induction tasks, achieving gains of up to 33 points and a median improvement of 10 points over the best automatic baselines.
elements.sent-2411-264: In summary, our contributions are threefold: (1) An RL formulation of instruction learning: We propose a continuous-action actor-critic RL approach for instruction optimization in black-box LLMs, enabling scalable exploration of infinite instruction spaces using entropy-regularized policies.. (2) Comprehensive validation.
elements.sent-2411-265: Across 33 diverse tasks, including instruction induction, zero-shot CoT reasoning, and summarization, ACING consistently outperforms both human-written and strong automated instructions, achieving statistically significant gains.
elements.sent-2411-266: Additionally, human evaluation and automated readability analysis confirm the clarity and semantic faithfulness of the generated instructions.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[scale=0.34]{figures/ACING_diagram_.pdf} \caption{ Pipeline of ACING. At each iteration, a soft prompt and task exemplars are fed to the white-box model to generate an instruction. This instruction queries the black-box LLM, whose outputs are scored. The resulting score is returned to the agent as a reward, which is used to update its networks and adjust its policy. Both LLMs remain frozen throughout.} \label{fig:ACING}
elements.subsection-2411-013: Problem: Prompt Optimization for Black-Box LLMs
elements.sent-2411-267: More formally, the objective is to maximize the expected score of the LLM in solving the task represented by the distribution $\mathcal{D}$, defined as: \begin{equation} \label{discrete_problem} \max_\tau \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-268: We utilize a validation dataset $\mathcal{V = \{(x_j, y_j)\}_{j=1 ^{m}$, where each pair consists of an input sentence $x_j$ and its corresponding ground truth output $y_j$.}}
elements.subsection-2411-014: Reformulating Discrete Prompt Search as a Continuous Optimization Problem
elements.sent-2411-269: To mitigate this challenge, similar to prior approaches \cite{instruct, instinct, hu2024localized we employ a publicly available, open-source white-box model, represented by $h$, and introduce a soft prompt vector $\mathbf{z \in \mathbb{R ^d$, which is a continuous $d$-dimensional vector representing the token embedding of a set of virtual tokens.}}}
elements.sent-2411-270: The white-box model $h$, which remains entirely frozen, with no training or gradient updates, serves as a proxy mapping $\mathbf{z}$ into a discrete prompt $\tau$ for the black-box LLM.
elements.sent-2411-271: Given a dataset of exemplars, $\mathcal{E = \{ (u_j, v_j) \}_{j=1 ^k$, where each pair $(u_j, v_j)$ defines input-output text sequences that exemplify a downstream task and the vector $\mathbf{z}$, their concatenation is input to the white-box model, generating the discrete prompt $\tau(\mathbf{z ) = h(\mathbf{z , \mathcal{E )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: The output $\hat{y _j$ is then evaluated using the scoring function $q(\hat{y _j, y_j)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-273: By using a fixed set of exemplars $\mathcal{E}$, the original discrete problem (Eq. (\ref{discrete_problem )) of finding the optimal prompt $\tau$ is effectively transformed into a continuous optimization problem over the soft prompt vector $\mathbf{z}$, as follows: \begin{equation} \label{continious_problem} \max_{\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^d} \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau(\mathbf{z}) \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-274: The soft prompt $\mathbf{z}$ is typically high-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-275: Specifically, we sample a matrix $P \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d'}$ with entries from $\text{Uniform (-1, 1)$, and optimize a lower-dimensional vector $\mathbf{a \in [0, 1]^{d'}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: The soft prompt is then given by $\mathbf{z = P\mathbf{a}$, transforming the original problem into optimization over a compact and continuous space as follows: \begin{equation} \label{continious_problem_} \max_{\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{d^{'}}} \quad \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[q(y, f(\tau(P\mathbf{a}) \oplus x))\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-277: This optimization problem is central to our framework.
elements.sent-2411-278: The following section elaborates on our approach to solving it.
elements.sent-2411-279: For clarity, we summarize all symbols used in our actor-critic framework in Table~\ref{tab:notations .}
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[scale=0.44]{figures/animal_example.pdf} \caption{ Illustration of the prompt generation and testing inside the environment using the larger\_animal dataset as an example.} \label{fig:animal_ACING}
elements.sent-2411-280: We formulate the problem of prompt learning for black-box LLMs as a RL problem, where the agent explores an infinite instruction space by sampling continuous actions $\mathbf{a \in [0,1]^{d^{' }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-281: Each action corresponds to a latent prompt representation, which is mapped—via a fixed projection matrix and a decoder—into a discrete instruction.
elements.sent-2411-282: This instruction is then evaluated by the black-box LLM on a validation set to produce a reward indicating task performance.
elements.sent-2411-283: The stateless, stochastic setup places the problem in the continuum bandit regime, unlike traditional discrete multi-armed bandits \cite{slivkins2019introduction, lattimore2020bandit .}
elements.sent-2411-284: To efficiently learn in a high-dimensional and limited-feedback setting, we propose a stateless, off-policy actor-critic framework inspired by recent advances in continuous control \cite{konda1999actor, pmlr-v48-mniha16, haarnoja2018soft, haarnoja2018softapp .}
elements.sent-2411-285: Our framework comprises lightweight networks: (1) a policy network (actor) $\pi(\cdot; \theta)$, which outputs latent action vectors corresponding to soft prompts, and (2) two critics to compute a value function $Q(\mathbf{a ) = \min\{Q_{\mathbf{w_1 (\mathbf{a ), Q_{\mathbf{w_2 (\mathbf{a )\}$, which estimates the expected reward of a given action.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: Formally, the policy $\pi(\cdot; \theta)$ is modeled as a Gaussian distribution with a diagonal covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-287: The actor network outputs the mean and log standard deviation for each action dimension, and actions are sampled using the reparameterization trick.
elements.sent-2411-288: The critics, $Q_{\mathbf{w_1 (.)$ and $Q_{\mathbf{w_2 (.)$, are trained to minimize the mean squared error between predicted and observed rewards: \begin{equation} \label{critic_loss} \min_{\mathbf{w}} J_Q(\mathbf{w}) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{a} \sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}\right)-r\left(\mathbf{a}\right) \right)^2\right],\end{equation} which can be optimized with stochastic gradients \begin{equation} \label{critic_gradient} \hat{\nabla}_{\mathbf{w}} J_Q(\mathbf{w})=\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)\left(Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)-r\left(\mathbf{a}_t\right)\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Training alternates between actor and critic updates.
elements.sent-2411-290: Despite the lack of state transitions, this architecture proves effective: the critic generalizes reward signals in prompt space, stabilizes updates by reducing reward variance, and enhances robustness via a twin-critic setup to mitigate overestimation.
elements.sent-2411-291: Empirically, this actor-critic formulation consistently outperforms no-critic baselines.
elements.sent-2411-292: In our setting, the agent operates under a fixed evaluation budget of $T$ queries and must discover the best-performing instruction within this limit.
elements.sent-2411-293: This corresponds to a pure exploration regime, where broad coverage of the action space is essential.
elements.sent-2411-294: While random exploration may seem sufficient, effective learning often requires balancing exploration with exploitation of prior observations to guide the search toward high-reward regions.
elements.sent-2411-295: To encourage systematic exploration while leveraging past experience, we adopt the maximum entropy reinforcement learning framework \cite{ziebart2010modeling, haarnoja2018soft .}
elements.sent-2411-296: This approach augments the expected reward with a policy entropy term, promoting stochasticity in the actor’s decisions.
elements.sent-2411-297: The objective becomes: \begin{equation} \label{policy_loss} \min_{\theta} J_\pi(\theta) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{a} \sim \pi(.;\theta)}\left[\alpha \log \left(\pi\left(\mathbf{a}; \theta\right)\right)-Q_{\mathbf{w}}\left(\mathbf{a})\right)\right],\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a temperature coefficient that governs the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-298: A higher $\alpha$ encourages greater policy entropy, while a lower value biases the policy toward exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-299: This formulation simplifies the soft actor-critic objective \cite{haarnoja2018soft , omitting state-related components and long-horizon returns, since our environment is stateless and rewards are immediate.}
elements.sent-2411-300: The entropy term, $-\mathbb{E _{\mathbf{a \sim \pi(\cdot;\theta) [\log \pi(\mathbf{a )]$, incentivizes the actor to maintain a diverse action distribution, thereby avoiding premature convergence to suboptimal prompts.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-301: To avoid manual tuning of $\alpha$, we follow prior work \cite{haarnoja2018softapp and adapt it to match a target entropy $H_{\text{target }$, by minimizing: \begin{equation} \label{alpha_loss} \min_{\alpha} \mathbf{J}_{\alpha} \triangleq -\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(.)} \left[ \alpha \cdot \left( \log \pi(\mathbf{a};\theta) + H_{\text{target}} \right) \right].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-302: We use stochastic gradient descent, specifically the Adam optimizer \cite{kingma2014adam, reddi2019convergence , to jointly update the policy, critic, and entropy temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING illustrates our actor-critic framework and its interaction with the environment.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Fig.~\ref{fig:animal_ACING zooms in on an example from the larger\_animal dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Overview: In each iteration \( t \leq T\), the actor-critic agent generates a continuous vector ``action'' \(\mathbf{a}\) (step 1).
elements.sent-2411-306: The action is then projected into the appropriate space using a fixed matrix $P$ to obtain \(\mathbf{z}\).
elements.sent-2411-307: The environment then concatenates the projected vector \(\mathbf{z}\) with a set of exemplars' embeddings from \(\mathcal{E}\) and feeds it into a white-box model $h$ (step 2).
elements.sent-2411-308: The white-box model produces a discrete prompt, \(\tau\), which is evaluated using the validation dataset \(\mathcal{V}\) based on the responses from the black-box LLM $f$ (step 3).
elements.sent-2411-309: The black-box LLM's prediction is compared to true labels of the validation examples, and a score function provides a reward, used to update both the critic and actor networks accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-311: The actor outputs the mean and variance of a distribution from which the action, a soft prompt vector $\mathbf{a}$ $\in$ $\mathbb{R ^{d^{' }$, is sampled.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: It also computes the log probability, which is key for policy optimization, as shown in Eq.~(\ref{policy_loss ).}
elements.sent-2411-314: As shown in the left side of Fig.~\ref{fig:animal_ACING , the examples describing the task from the set of exemplars $\mathcal{E}$, along with additional text such as ``The instruction was to,'' are input into the embedding layer of the white-box model to generate continuous vectors (using the instruction generation template in Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING top right).}}
elements.sent-2411-315: The white-box model layers subsequently process the resulting concatenated vector to produce the discrete prompt $\tau$, suitable for input into the black-box LLM.
elements.sent-2411-317: The overall score is calculated by averaging the scores across all samples, representing the reward: $r = \frac{1 {m \sum_{i=1 ^m q(\hat{y _i, y_i)$, where $m$ represents the number of samples.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: The critic evaluates the actions taken by the actor using the network $Q_{w}$, which estimates the expected reward for a generated action $\mathbf{a}$ from the policy network $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-320: Based on the observed reward $r(\mathbf{a )$, the critic updates its network using the loss function (Eq.(\ref{critic_loss )) and gradient (Eq.(\ref{critic_gradient )).}}}
elements.sent-2411-321: The critic’s feedback helps the actor improve its policy by maximizing the reward (Eq.~(\ref{policy_loss )), ensuring a balance between exploration and exploitation.}
elements.sent-2411-322: After \(T\) iterations, the agent returns the best-performing prompt \(\tau^\star\), which is evaluated on the test set \(\mathcal{T}\) in the black-box LLM using the evaluation template shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:ACING (bottom right).}

elements.sent-2411-403: ACING jointly addresses both the generation and selection in a unified framework, yielding high-performing prompts without pool constraints or external supervision.
elements.sent-2411-404: We present ACING, an actor-critic RL framework for prompt optimization in black-box LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-405: By formulating the task as a continuous-action problem, ACING enables exploration of an infinite space through an entropy-regularized policy under strict query constraints.
elements.sent-2411-406: It outperforms strong baselines and surpasses human-written instructions, without any per-task tuning, demonstrating effectiveness, efficiency, and practicality.
elements.sent-2411-407: While ACING achieves strong and consistent performance across diverse tasks, we do not claim universal superiority on every individual task.
elements.sent-2411-408: Given the stochastic and non-convex nature of prompt optimization—particularly in black-box LLMs under tight query budgets—strong prompts can occasionally arise even from random search or other optimization approaches.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, ACING substantially improves the chances of discovering such prompts through a principled exploration strategy grounded in actor-critic learning.
elements.sent-2411-410: Similar to previous prior works \citep{instinct, instruct, hu2024localized , our reliance on a white-box model introduces variability, as model selection affects performance (Appendix~\ref{different_white_llms ).}}
elements.sent-2411-411: Addressing this limitation would require operating directly on black-box LLMs, which poses challenges due to the large discrete action space.
elements.sent-2411-412: Adapting RL methods for such spaces, as explored in \citet{pmlr-v235-fourati24a , and proposing hybrid approaches combining soft-prompt and discrete optimization, represent promising directions for future research.}
elements.sent-2411-413: Despite the strong performance achieved with a fixed hyperparameter configuration across all tasks, we acknowledge that domain-specific tuning might yield marginal gains, particularly in edge cases.
elements.sent-2411-414: That said, our goal was to emphasize generality and simplicity without introducing per-task overhead—an important factor for real-world usability.
elements.sent-2411-415: ACING automates prompt optimization for LLMs, which can reduce manual effort but may also amplify risks associated with bias, misuse, or harmful content generation.
elements.sent-2411-416: We emphasize that ACING is a general optimization framework and does not guarantee ethical outputs from the underlying LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-417: It should be paired with appropriate content filters and safety mechanisms when deployed in sensitive domains.
elements.sent-2411-418: Optimized prompts could reinforce or amplify existing biases present in the base LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-419: Future work should incorporate fairness-aware reward functions or post-hoc bias mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-420: Lastly, our experiments assume legal and ethical API usage, and we caution against applying instruction optimization to restricted or proprietary models without adherence to usage policies and terms of service.. .

elements.subsection-2411-022: Data Usage, Privacy, and Safety Considerations
elements.sent-2411-442: All datasets for the studied tasks are publicly released and widely used in NLP research.
elements.sent-2411-443: We used them under their respective licenses for academic use, and we cite the original sources in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-444: None of the datasets we used contain personally identifiable information (PII) to the best of our knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-445: The datasets are either synthetic, anonymized, or derived from public sources with appropriate preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-446: We did not augment or modify these datasets in ways that would introduce identifying information.
elements.sent-2411-447: For our human evaluation of ACING-generated prompts, we used only automatically generated instructions and task templates.
elements.sent-2411-448: Prior to annotation, we reviewed the generated prompts and outputs to ensure that they did not contain any offensive or inappropriate content.
elements.sent-2411-449: The tasks involved abstract or anonymized content (e.g., generic input strings or public task descriptions), and no human names, images, or personal data were included.
elements.sent-2411-450: We ensured that all experiments involving human subjects were conducted ethically and responsibly.
elements.sent-2411-451: Participation in the annotation study was fully voluntary.
elements.sent-2411-452: Annotators were informed about the nature and purpose of the task and could withdraw at any time.
elements.sent-2411-453: The study was limited to rating task-level clarity and alignment, involved no sensitive content, and did not collect any personal or identifiable information.
elements.sent-2411-454: All responses were anonymized, and only aggregate statistics were analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-455: As the study posed no foreseeable risk to participants and did not involve identifiable data, it did not require formal ethics review under our institution’s guidelines.

elements.sent-2411-459: Summarization: Dialogue-based abstractive summarization using the SAMSum dataset \citep{gliwa2019samsum .}
elements.sent-2411-460: Translation: Lexical-level English-to-German, English-to-Spanish, and English-to-French tasks to assess multilingual instruction learning.
elements.sent-2411-461: Our experiments involve four languages: English (the primary language for most tasks), and German, Spanish, and French in translation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-462: The tasks cover a broad range of linguistic phenomena, including:.
elements.sent-2411-464: Lexical Semantics: e.g., antonyms, synonyms, word similarity.
elements.sent-2411-465: Discourse and Pragmatics: e.g., summarization, cause-effect reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-466: Symbolic and Numerical Reasoning: e.g., math problem solving, program induction.

elements.subsection-2411-026: On Direct Parameterization of the Actor
elements.sent-2411-517: To assess the impact of using a feed-forward network (FFN) versus direct parameterization for the actor, we compare the following two variants:.
elements.sent-2411-518: ACING: Our standard approach, where the actor is parameterized using a feed-forward neural network that receives a constant one-dimensional input (set to 1).
elements.sent-2411-519: ACING (direct parameterization): A variant using the same critics, but with the actor represented directly by learnable mean and variance vectors, completely removing the FFN.
elements.sent-2411-520: We report results averaged across 30 diverse tasks (3 trials per task), summarizing performance by task category and global statistics.
elements.sent-2411-521: Summary: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:acing_vs_parametrized , the FFN-based ACING achieves a substantially higher median score (0.69 vs. 0.44) and outperforms the direct parameterization variant on many more tasks (19 vs. 8).}
elements.sent-2411-522: While direct parameterization occasionally matches or surpasses FFN performance on simpler tasks (e.g., Negation, Antonyms, Num\_to\_verbal, Informal\_to\_formal), it generally struggles on tasks requiring more compositional or fine-grained control (e.g., Word\_sorting, Odd\_one\_out, Auto\_categorization).

elements.section-2411-024: Assessing Instruction Clarity: Human and Readability Analyses
elements.sent-2411-532: To assess the interpretability and accessibility of the instructions generated by ACING, we conduct both a human evaluation and an automated readability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-533: We conducted a human annotation study across all instruction-induction tasks reported in \cref{tab:human_performance_main .}
elements.sent-2411-534: A total of 26 participants (none of whom are paper co-authors) volunteered to independently rate the clarity, coherence, and task faithfulness of instructions produced by ACING using a 5-point Likert scale.
elements.sent-2411-535: The following question was used to guide ratings:.
elements.sent-2411-536: Does the instruction clearly describe what is happening in the demonstration? Could a language model complete the task correctly by following this instruction alone?.
elements.sent-2411-541: The results of the human evaluation are summarized below:.
elements.sent-2411-547: These results indicate that the majority of instructions are perceived as clear and well-aligned with the task by human annotators.
elements.sent-2411-548: To complement the human study, we applied three established readability formulas to quantify the linguistic accessibility of the 30 generated instructions in \cref{tbl:best_prompt_instruction_induction :.}
elements.sent-2411-549: Flesch Reading Ease (FRE) \cite{flesch1948new : Scores range from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating greater ease of reading.}
elements.sent-2411-550: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (FKG) \cite{kincaid1975derivation : Maps readability to U.S.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Coleman-Liau Index (CLI) \cite{coleman1975computer : A character-based grade-level readability metric.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Readability scores across instructions show the following trends:.
elements.sent-2411-553: Most instructions fall within the FRE range of 60–95, with a median 70.8, indicating that they are accessible to a general audience.
elements.sent-2411-554: FKG scores mostly range between 3 and 9, with a median 7.0, consistent with middle school to early high school reading levels.
elements.sent-2411-555: CLI scores mostly range between 2–10, with a median of 7.3.
elements.sent-2411-556: This aligns with the FKG analysis, indicating that instructions are generally suitable for readers with middle school to early high school reading levels.
elements.sent-2411-557: Overall, these results confirm that ACING generates instructions that are not only effective—according to human judgment—but also accessible, as measured by standardized readability metrics.
elements.sent-2411-558: The full set of readability scores is included in \cref{tab:readability_scores_full .}

elements.sent-2411-559: In this section, we present the best-learned instructions discovered by ACING for each of the 30 instruction induction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-560: These instructions were generated using Vicuna-13B as the white-box model and optimized to maximize performance on the corresponding black-box evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-561: Table~\ref{tbl:best_prompt_instruction_induction showcases the resulting instructions alongside their test scores, which reflect the accuracy or task-specific metric obtained on held-out examples.}
elements.sent-2411-562: The diversity and clarity of the instructions demonstrate ACING’s ability to synthesize task-relevant, semantically grounded prompts that elicit strong responses from black-box LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-563: Notably, several tasks achieve perfect scores, while others expose task-specific challenges (e.g., common\_concept and synonyms), highlighting the varying difficulty across the instruction spectrum.
